title: Global tokio RwLock health map (plus lb_state write lock) acquired on every client message
currentEvidence: server.rs:1218 `let health = state.health.read().await;` (also 1224, 1592, 1644) — acquired per query in choose_target_node/select_read_node. The lb_state half is fixed: line 213 `rr_counter: AtomicU64` and line 1660 `state.lb_state.rr_counter.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed)` — no write lock.
fix: Replace `health: RwLock<HashMap<String, NodeHealth>>` (server.rs:141) with `health: arc_swap::ArcSwap<HashMap<String, NodeHealth>>` (arc-swap already idiomatic for read-mostly maps). On the routing path (choose_target_node 1218/1224, select_read_node 1644, select_primary_with_timeout 1592, select_node 2205, plus the admin sync at 627), replace `state.health.read().await` with a lock-free `let health = state.health.load();` (returns a `Guard<Arc<HashMap>>`, single relaxed atomic load, no await, no semaphore). In the background check_node_health task (2271), instead of `.write().await` + in-place mutation, build a new HashMap (clone-and-modify the current snapshot) and `state.health.store(Arc::new(new_map))`. This removes the await point and the writer-fairness semaphore from every per-query route decision while keeping identical semantics (readers see a consistent snapshot; the periodic checker is the sole writer). The lb_state portion of the finding needs no change — already lock-free.
reasoning: The finding has two halves. (1) "lb_state write lock" is OBSOLETE — Batch A converted rr_counter to AtomicU64 (line 213) and the read path now uses fetch_add with Relaxed ordering (line 1660); LoadBalancerState contains only the atomic, no lock. (2) "Global tokio RwLock health map acquired on every client message" is STILL PRESENT and real: the per-message hot path (forward_simple_query->choose_target_node at 1331, forward_extended_batch->choose_target_node at 1370) takes state.health.read().await at 1218/1224, and on a route switch select_read_node (1644) / select_primary_with_timeout (1592) take it again. The map is read-mostly — the only writer is the periodic background health checker (2271), never the data path — which is exactly the workload where a tokio RwLock is suboptimal: every reader still touches the shared semaphore and crosses an await point even with zero real contention, and tokio's writer-fairness adds overhead under many concurrent sessions. It is on the default hot path (runs for every query regardless of optional features). Switching to ArcSwap turns each route decision's health lookup into one relaxed atomic load with no await and no semaphore, preserving snapshot semantics. Modest but legitimate per-query win; effort M because it touches ~6 read sites plus the writer task. Confidence high — all call sites read directly from current code.

title: Per-message full-buffer clone and double-decode in backend response loop (O(n2) in response size)
currentEvidence: server.rs:1452-1459 `if consumed > 0 { client.write_all(&buf[..consumed]).await...; sent += consumed as u64; let _ = buf.split_to(consumed); }` — frames are walked by byte offset (1427-1450), written once, and the consumed prefix is split off; no full-buffer clone, single decode pass.

The backend response relay is now `stream_until_ready` (src/server.rs:1411-1480), which coalesces complete frames into one `write_all(&buf[..consumed])`, splits off consumed bytes, and decodes each frame exactly once (O(response) total, O(frame) memory). `forward_simple_query` (1341) and `forward_extended_batch` (1390-1393) both call it. A grep of the entire src tree finds no `response_buffer.clone()` / response-buffer clone. The finding's "ha-failover-replay" subsystem tag is a misattribution: src/replay/mod.rs and src/failover_replay.rs relay via BackendClient (tokio-postgres), not raw wire buffers; their `.clone()` calls are config/journal-entry clones, not per-message response clones.
reasoning: The cited pattern (per-message full-buffer clone + double-decode, O(n^2) in response size) is the pre-Batch-A backend response relay. The current code in src/server.rs implements exactly the described Batch-A fix: stream_until_ready walks frames in place using length-prefix offsets, performs one coalesced write of all complete frames, splits off the consumed prefix, and never clones the whole buffer nor decodes twice. Memory is O(frame), CPU O(response). Although the backend response relay IS on the default hot path (every query response flows through it), the issue no longer exists, so there is nothing to fix and no further perf gain available here.

The global RwLock (line 239) is acquired for write on every begin/log/savepoint/commit, and log_statement calls total_size() (line 346) which is an O(n) re-scan of all prior entries (lines 213-218), giving per-transaction O(n^2) journaling. title: Failover detection latency floor: TCP-connect-only health probes run serially, and blocked writers poll at 500 ms
currentEvidence: All three sub-issues confirmed present and untouched by Batch A-D. (2) Serial probing, server.rs:2269 `for node in &config.nodes {` then 2270 `let result = Self::check_node_health(node, config).await;` — each awaited sequentially, write lock taken per-node at 2271. (3) 500ms writer poll, server.rs:1588 `let check_interval = Duration::from_millis(500);` and 1623 `tokio::time::sleep(check_interval).await;` inside `select_primary_with_timeout`, which IS wired on the write path at 1247 `Self::select_primary_with_timeout(session, state, config).await`. fix:
Three independent, additive changes in src/server.rs (none on steady-state per-query path):

1. Parallelize the health sweep (check_all_nodes, ~2268-2298): build a Vec of futures `config.nodes.iter().map(|n| async move { (n.address(), Self::check_node_health(n, config).await) })` and drive with `futures::future::join_all(...).await`, then take the health write lock ONCE and apply all results. Removes the N×check_timeout serial floor and the per-node lock churn. Low risk; pure scheduling change.

2. Make the probe protocol-aware (check_node_health, 2301-2315): after TcpStream::connect, send a PG startup/SSLRequest or run a trivial `SELECT 1` round-trip (the codebase already has backend auth + frame helpers used by ensure_conn) and require a successful response before returning Ok. This closes the false-healthy gap where a backend accepts TCP but is not serving SQL. Medium risk (needs a lightweight auth/query helper or reuse of existing backend handshake), but it is the correctness-critical part.

3. Shorten / event-drive the writer poll (select_primary_with_timeout, 1588/1623): either drop check_interval to e.g. 50ms, or (cleaner) replace the polling loop with a tokio watch/Notify that the health checker fires when a node's `healthy` flag flips, so a blocked writer is released immediately on promotion instead of up to 500ms later. The 50ms knob is the minimal change; the Notify is the principled one.

Optionally expose check_interval_secs/failure_threshold as the real detection-floor knobs in docs, since default 3×5s dominates the 500ms.

All three described behaviors are present in the CURRENT code and were not altered by Batch A-D (which targeted query-relay hot paths, SQL classification, plugin runtime, and connection caching — none touched HA timing). The code does exactly what the finding claims: check_node_health (2301-2315) is TCP-connect-only with a code comment admitting the health query is unimplemented; check_all_nodes (2268-2298) probes nodes serially with a per-node write-lock; and select_primary_with_timeout (1580-1625) polls every 500ms via tokio::time::sleep and is genuinely wired on the write path (choose_target_node line 1247).

The dominant detection floor is failure_threshold(3) × check_interval(5s) ≈ 15s plus connect timeout, and the serial sweep adds up to N×check_timeout(3s) more with many nodes; the 500ms writer poll adds tail latency to an already-blocked writer after promotion; and the TCP-only probe is a correctness bug (false-healthy backend that accepts TCP but cannot serve SQL — e.g. crash-recovering Postgres). onDefaultHotPath=false: the health checker is a background task and select_primary_with_timeout only enters its sleep loop during an actual failover window (when a switch is needed AND the primary is unhealthy). For tenant, use Cow so the IP-fallback path can still own a String while the multi-tenancy path borrows; the per-query `entry(ctx.tenant.clone())` map insert at mod.rs:167 still needs an owned key, but that clone only materializes a String when the tenant row is first created (or can use the raw-entry/entry_ref API to avoid cloning on the hit path). fingerprint must stay materialized (it's a String built by anomaly_fingerprint and used as a HashMap key), so keep it owned. Net: removes the largest of the three allocations (full SQL copy) on every observed query with no behavior change. Only worthwhile under builds with anomaly-detection enabled.
reasoning: The finding conflates two distinct allocations. The RFC3339 string is NOT eagerly allocated in current code — Batch A moved it strictly inside each detector's firing branch (anomaly/mod.rs:182/198/219), so that half of the finding is already fixed. The "three owned Strings" half IS still present: record_anomaly_sql (server.rs:1946-1971) constructs a QueryObservation per query with tenant (owned String), fingerprint (owned String from a with_capacity scan), and sql: query.to_string() (a full copy of the SQL) BEFORE record_query decides whether any detector fires. Inside record_query, sql is only ever read by-reference (excerpt, sql_injection::scan), so the sql.to_string() copy is pure waste on the no-fire path; fingerprint is genuinely needed as a HashMap key; tenant is cloned again for the rate-window key. This sits on the per-query path (called unconditionally at server.rs:816 for every Query and server.rs:886 for every Parse, ahead of the plugin hook and forwarding) — but only when the anomaly-detection feature is compiled in. Cargo.toml shows default = ["pool-modes"]; anomaly-detection (line 94) is empty/off by default and only bundled into the larger all-features group (line 114). So onDefaultHotPath is false, but matters is true for the feature-on configuration where the SQL copy scales with query size and rate.

title: Auth relay polls the client with a fixed 100ms timeout per round
currentEvidence: src/server.rs:1190 `let n = tokio::time::timeout(Duration::from_millis(100), client_stream.read(&mut read_buf)).await;` — inside the `loop` of `proxy_authentication` (1140-1202), reached on every iteration that did not already return on ReadyForQuery/ErrorResponse.
fix: Restructure proxy_authentication (src/server.rs:1131-1203) into an event-driven relay instead of a fixed 100ms poll. Concretely: after forwarding backend bytes and draining frames, only read from the client when the last decoded backend frame was an AuthRequest that actually demands a client response (CleartextPassword=3, Md5Password=5, SASL=10, SASLContinue=11) — and in that case use `client_stream.read()` with NO artificial timeout (or a generous overall auth deadline, e.g. tokio::time::timeout(config.pool.acquire_timeout(), ...) wrapping the whole function). When the last backend frame was AuthenticationOk (auth_type==0) or any non-challenge message, loop straight back to the blocking backend read without touching the client at all. The cleanest form is `tokio::select!` over `backend_stream.read()` and `client_stream.read()` so each side is relayed as soon as bytes arrive, with a single outer deadline for the whole handshake. This eliminates the per-round 100ms wait (currently incurred whenever AuthenticationOk and ReadyForQuery arrive in separate reads, and during SASL inter-challenge gaps) while preserving the pass-through password forwarding.
reasoning: The finding is accurate and the code is unchanged by batches A-D. `proxy_authentication` is on the default, non-feature-gated path: every client connection runs connect_and_authenticate -> proxy_authentication (callers at server.rs:1023, 1051). The loop reads from backend (blocking, line 1142), forwards to client, drains frames, and returns immediately on ReadyForQuery (1176) or ErrorResponse (1180). If neither terminal frame is present in the current buffer, it falls through to line 1190 and waits up to 100ms for a client password response. The penalty is real but bounded at 100ms per round and only materializes when the client has nothing to send: chiefly when AuthenticationOk arrives in a separate TCP read from ReadyForQuery (note the auth_type==0 branch at 1169 is a no-op and does NOT short-circuit), and during multi-round SASL gaps. For typical trust/password auth where PG coalesces AuthenticationOk+ParameterStatus+BackendKeyData+ReadyForQuery into one segment, the timeout is often not hit, so it is not a guaranteed 100ms tax on every connection. But under split reads or SCRAM it adds up to ~100ms of one-time connection-setup latency, which matters for connection churn / short-lived clients. It does not affect steady-state per-query throughput. matters=true and onDefaultHotPath=true because connection establishment is part of the core path, though the magnitude is one-time-per-connection, not per-query. A select!-based event-driven relay removes it cleanly.

title: Hook registry clones a Vec<String> of plugin names under the lock for every hook stage of every query
currentEvidence: src/plugins/mod.rs:474 `let plugin_names = hooks.get(&HookType::PreQuery).cloned().unwrap_or_default();` (same pattern at :523 post_query, :574 authenticate, :618 route). Registry field at :356 `hooks: RwLock<HashMap<HookType, Vec<String>>>`.
fix: Optional, low value. To eliminate the per-call Vec<String> clone, change the registry to RwLock<HashMap<HookType, Arc<Vec<String>>>> (mod.rs:356). In each execute_* method (mod.rs:474/523/574/618) replace `.cloned().unwrap_or_default()` with `.map(Arc::clone)` and iterate `&*plugin_names`, so only an Arc refcount bump happens under the lock instead of deep-copying the strings. Update load_plugin/unload_plugin (mod.rs:407-413, 432-435) to rebuild the Arc on mutation (copy-on-write). Do NOT instead hold the read lock across the loop — that would hold it across the WASM call and block hot-reload/load/unload writers.
reasoning: The described code is still present and does exactly what the finding claims: execute_pre_query/post_query/authenticate/route each call `hooks.get(&hook).cloned()` which deep-copies a Vec<String> of plugin names under the parking_lot read lock. However it does not matter on any realistic path. (1) The entire plugins subsystem is feature-gated behind `wasm-plugins` (Cargo.toml:80) — with the feature off, none of this compiles, so it is NOT on the default per-query/connection hot path. (2) Even with the feature ON, Batch A added has_hook() (mod.rs:464) and wired every per-query call site to guard with it: server.rs:1893 (pre_query), :2109 (route), :2159 (post_query) all `return` early before calling execute_* when no plugin is registered for that hook — and has_hook does NOT clone (it checks `!names.is_empty()` under the read lock). So the `.cloned()` only runs when a plugin is genuinely subscribed to that hook, in which case a full wasmtime call follows and dominates cost; cloning a handful of short Strings is negligible beside it. execute_authenticate is per-connection, not per-query. Net: real but immaterial; the Arc-clone fix is a small correctness-preserving micro-optimization, not a throughput win on any wired data path.

title: Plugins only hook the simple-query protocol: extended-protocol traffic (Parse/Bind/Execute) bypasses all hooks
currentEvidence: server.rs:1887 `if msg.msg_type != MessageType::Query { return (msg, PreQueryAction::Forward); }` in apply_pre_query_hook; doc at 1872-1874 "Only simple-query (MessageType::Query) messages are inspected today. Extended-protocol messages (Parse/Bind/Execute) are passed through unchanged"; the Parse|Bind|Execute branch (869-890) and Sync|Flush branch calling forward_extended_batch (897-908) invoke no PreQuery/PostQuery hook; forward_extended_batch (1356-1402) has zero hook calls.
fix: Wire the PreQuery hook into the extended-protocol Parse handling and PostQuery into the Sync boundary, in src/server.rs client_loop. The routing SQL is already extracted at lines 877-883 into pending_route_sql; reuse it: when a Parse first sets pending_route_sql (and pm.has_hook(PreQuery)), build a QueryContext via build_query_context and call pm.execute_pre_query. On Block, send_block_response, drop the accumulated `pending` batch, and skip forwarding until the next Sync resets state (must also emit an ErrorResponse for the in-flight extended sequence and swallow client frames until Sync). On Rewrite, re-encode the Parse payload with the new SQL into `pending`. After forward_extended_batch returns at the Sync boundary (lines 897-919), call fire_post_query_hook with the route_sql and the ForwardResult-equivalent. Note this is a correctness/coverage fix (extended-protocol clients currently bypass column-mask, guardrail, cost-governor, residency gating), NOT a performance fix; it adds work rather than removing it. Keep it behind the existing has_hook fast-path so the no-plugin case stays zero-cost.
reasoning: The finding is fully present and accurate in current code. apply_pre_query_hook short-circuits any non-Query message (server.rs:1887), and its own doc comment (1872-1874) admits extended-protocol is "passed through unchanged — a future task wires them in." The hook call sites (820, 859) are exclusively inside the MessageType::Query arm; the Parse/Bind/Execute arm (869-890) only feeds the anomaly detector and accumulates bytes, and forward_extended_batch (1356-1402) does pure routing+stream with no hook calls. Batches A-D were latency/throughput work (TCP_NODELAY, alloc-free classification, frame streaming, conn cache, plugin runtime caching); none added extended-protocol hook coverage, so the gap is unchanged. matters=false and onDefaultHotPath=false under the schema's performance definition: this is a security/correctness coverage gap (most real drivers use the extended protocol and would silently escape column-mask/guardrail/cost/residency plugins), and fixing it ADDS per-query work on the extended path rather than improving latency, throughput, or scalability. With no plugins loaded the has_hook fast path keeps the extended path untouched, so there is no performance regression to recover either. Confidence high — verified by reading the exact lines and all hook call sites.
